title: Vinegar pie
subtitle: American pioneer egg-and-vinegar custard pie
excerpt: An old American pie made without citrus: eggs, sugar, butter, and a splash of cider vinegar baked in a shortcrust shell to produce a custard with a bright, lemon-like edge. A resourceful recipe from an era when lemons were expensive and vinegar was not. Surprisingly good — more elegant than its name suggests.
sourceNotes: Vinegar pie is documented in American pioneer and Depression-era cookbooks as a substitute for lemon pie when citrus was unavailable or too expensive. Recipes appear in American domestic manuscript cookbooks from the nineteenth century and in regional American cookbooks of the early twentieth century, including Midwestern and Southern sources. The formula of eggs, sugar, butter, and cider or white vinegar baked in a pastry shell is consistent across these sources. The recipe here follows the established formula from nineteenth-century American domestic sources.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake and chill up to a day ahead. The filling firms overnight and the flavour improves.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 170°C. Bake the pastry case blind for 12 minutes before adding the filling. The filled pie is done when the custard is just set — it should wobble as one piece, not ripple in the centre, when the dish is gently shaken.

glossaryTerms.definition: Vinegar pie filling is a cooked egg-and-butter custard in which cider vinegar provides the acidity that lemon juice would in a lemon curd tart. The vinegar brightens the filling and prevents it from being cloying, without tasting sour in the finished pie. The critical doneness test is the wobble: a fully set vinegar pie custard moves as one piece when the dish is shaken, not in rippling waves at the centre.

content.text: Vinegar pie tastes brighter than its name suggests. The
is a simple custard — it does not curdle, does not need a bain-marie, and bakes in under 30 minutes once the shell is ready. Judge doneness by the wobble, not by time.

Rub the cold butter into the flour and salt until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Add the egg yolk and cold water and mix to a firm dough. Wrap and refrigerate for 20 minutes. Heat the oven to 190°C conventional. Roll the dough to 3 mm and line a 23 cm pie dish. Trim the edge, prick the base, and line with baking paper and dried beans. Blind bake for 12 minutes, then remove the weights and paper and bake for a further 5 minutes until the base is pale and dry. Reduce the oven to 170°C conventional.

Whisk the eggs and sugar together until combined. Add the cooled melted butter, cider vinegar, vanilla, and flour and whisk until smooth. Pour into the par-baked shell. Bake at 170°C for 25 to 30 minutes until the custard is just set — it should move as one piece when the dish is gently shaken, with no liquid wobble at the centre. Cool completely at room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 1 hour before slicing.
